content:
# Exodus Chapter 14 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~39v–40r (PDF pp. 76–77); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 54–55 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_014_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_014_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_014_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_014_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The LORD's Command to Encamp (vv. 1–4)

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**2** "Speak to the sons of Israel, that they turn back [**veyashuvu**] and encamp before Pi-hahiroth [**Pi haChirot**], between Migdol [**Migdol**] and the sea, before Baal-zephon [**Ba'al Tsefon**]; opposite it you shall encamp by the sea.

**3** And Pharaoh will say concerning the sons of Israel, 'They are wandering aimlessly [**nevukhim hem**] in the land; the wilderness has closed in on them [**sagar aleihem hammidbar**].'

**4** And I will strengthen [**vechizzaqti**, Piel] Pharaoh's heart [**et-lev Par'oh**], and he will pursue [**veradaf**] after them. And I will be glorified [**ve'ikkavdah**] through Pharaoh and through all his army [**uvkhol-cheilo**], and the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD." And they did so.

## Pharaoh Pursues Israel (vv. 5–9)

**5** And it was told to the king of Egypt that the people had fled [**barach ha'am**]. And the heart of Pharaoh and his servants was turned [**vayyehafekh**] against the people, and they said, "What is this we have done, that we have sent Israel away from serving us [**me'ovdenu**]?"

**6** And he harnessed [**vayyesor**] his chariot, and he took his people [**ammo**] with him.

**7** And he took six hundred chosen [**bachur**] chariots, and all the chariots of Egypt, and officers [**shalishim**] over all of them.

**8** And the LORD strengthened [**vayechazzeq**, Piel] the heart of Pharaoh, king of Egypt, and he pursued [**vayyirdof**] after the sons of Israel. And the sons of Israel were going out with a high hand [**beyad ramah**].

**9** And the Egyptians pursued after them and overtook [**vayyassigu**] them encamping by the sea — all the horses and chariots of Pharaoh, and his horsemen [**ufarashav**], and his army — by Pi-hahiroth, before Baal-zephon.

## Israel's Fear and Moses' Response (vv. 10–14)

**10** And Pharaoh drew near [**hiqriv**], and the sons of Israel lifted up their eyes, and behold, Egypt was marching [**nosea**] after them, and they feared greatly. And the sons of Israel cried out [**vayyits'aqu**] to the LORD.

**11** And they said to Moses, "Is it because there are no graves [**mibli ein-qevarim**] in Egypt that you have taken us to die in the wilderness? What is this you have done to us, bringing us out from Egypt?

**12** Is this not the word that we spoke to you in Egypt, saying, 'Leave us alone [**chadal mimmenu**], and let us serve [**vena'avdah**] Egypt'? For it would have been better for us to serve Egypt than to die in the wilderness."

**13** And Moses said to the people, "Do not fear [**al-tira'u**]. Stand firm [**hityatsvu**] and see [**ure'u**] the salvation of the LORD [**yeshu'at YHWH**] which He will do for you today. For the Egyptians whom you see today, you will never see them again [**lo tosifu lir'otam od**], unto eternity [**ad-olam**].

**14** The LORD will fight [**yillachem**] for you, and you shall be silent [**tacharishun**]."

## The LORD's Command to Move Forward (vv. 15–18)

**15** And the LORD said to Moses, "Why do you cry out [**mah-tits'aq**] to Me? Speak to the sons of Israel, and let them journey on [**veyissa'u**].

**16** And you, lift up [**harem**] your staff [**mattekha**] and stretch out [**uneteh**] your hand over the sea, and divide it [**uveqa'ehu**], and the sons of Israel will go through the midst of the sea [**betokh hayyam**] on dry ground [**bayyabbashah**].

**17** And I, behold, I am strengthening [**mechazzeq**, Piel] the heart of the Egyptians [**lev Mitsrayim**], and they will come in after them. And I will be glorified [**ve'ikkavdah**] through Pharaoh and through all his army, through his chariots and through his horsemen.

**18** And the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD, when I am glorified [**behikkavdi**] through Pharaoh, through his chariots, and through his horsemen."

## The Angel and the Pillar Move (vv. 19–20)

**19** And the angel of God [**mal'akh ha'Elohim**], the one going [**haholekh**] before the camp of Israel, journeyed and went behind them [**vayyeylekh me'acharehem**]. And the pillar of cloud [**ammud he'anan**] journeyed from before them and stood behind them [**vayyaammod me'acharehem**].

**20** And it came between the camp of Egypt and the camp of Israel. And there was the cloud and the darkness [**he'anan vehachoshekh**], and it lit up [**vayya'er**] the night. And the one did not come near [**lo-qarav zeh el-zeh**] the other all the night.

## The Dividing of the Sea (vv. 21–22)

**21** And Moses stretched out [**vayyeyt**] his hand over the sea, and the LORD caused the sea to go [**vayyolekh**] by a strong east wind [**beruach qadim azzah**] all the night, and He made the sea into dry land [**lecharavah**], and the waters were divided [**vayyibbaq'u hammayim**].

**22** And the sons of Israel went into the midst of the sea [**betokh hayyam**] on the dry ground [**bayyabbashah**], and the waters were a wall [**chomah**] to them on their right and on their left.

## The Egyptians Pursue into the Sea (vv. 23–25)

**23** And the Egyptians pursued and came in after them — all the horses of Pharaoh, his chariots, and his horsemen — into the midst of the sea.

**24** And it was in the morning watch [**be'ashmoret habboqer**], and the LORD looked down [**vayyashqef**] upon the camp of Egypt through the pillar of fire and cloud [**be'ammud esh va'anan**], and He threw into confusion [**vayyahom**] the camp of Egypt.

**25** And He turned aside [**vayyasar**] the wheels of his chariots, and He made them drive [**vaynahagehu**] with heaviness [**bikhvedut**]. And Egypt said, "Let me flee [**anusah**] from the face of Israel, for the LORD is fighting [**nilcham**] for them against Egypt."

## The Sea Returns (vv. 26–29)

**26** And the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch out [**neteh**] your hand over the sea, and the waters will return [**veyashuvu hammayim**] upon Egypt, upon its chariots, and upon its horsemen."

**27** And Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and the sea returned [**vayyashov hayyam**] at the turning of the morning [**lifnot boqer**] to its normal flow [**le'etano**]. And the Egyptians were fleeing [**nassim**] to meet it [**liqra'to**], and the LORD shook off [**vayena'er**] the Egyptians into the midst of the sea.

**28** And the waters returned and covered [**vayekhassu**] the chariots and the horsemen — all the army of Pharaoh that came after them into the sea. Not even one [**ad-echad**] remained among them.

**29** And the sons of Israel walked on the dry ground [**bayyabbashah**] in the midst of the sea, and the waters were a wall [**chomah**] to them on their right and on their left.

## The Great Salvation (vv. 30–31)

**30** And the LORD saved [**vayyosha**] Israel on that day from the hand of Egypt. And Israel saw [**vayyar**] Egypt dead upon the shore [**sefat**] of the sea.

**31** **And Israel saw [**vayyar**] the great hand [**hayyad haggedolah**] which the LORD had done [**asah**] against Egypt. And the people feared [**vayyir'u**] the LORD, and they believed [**vayya'aminu**] in the LORD and in Moses His servant [**avdo**].**

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folios ~39v–40r (PDF pp. 76–77) contain the entirety of Exodus 14 in the standard three-column layout with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks. The chapter begins on the first page (page 76, continuing from the end of chapter 13) and extends through the second page (page 77). A petuchah (open paragraph) marker is visible before 14:1, indicating a major thematic division — the shift from the wilderness-route narrative (13:17–22) to the Sea of Reeds confrontation. This petuchah marker signals that 14:1 opens an entirely new narrative unit. A setumah (closed paragraph) marker appears before v. 15, where the LORD's speech shifts from consolation-through-Moses to the command for action ("Why do you cry out to Me?"). Another setumah appears before v. 19, marking the transition from divine command to the movement of the angel and the pillar of cloud. A significant setumah marker appears before v. 26, introducing the climactic reversal — the command for the waters to return. The masorah parva annotations are visible throughout, including notations on the term nevukhim (v. 3, "wandering"), on the phrase beyad ramah (v. 8, "with a high hand"), on the unique phrasing vayya'er et-hallaylah (v. 20, "and it lit up the night"), and on le'etano (v. 27, "to its normal flow"). The cantillation on v. 14 places distinctive emphasis on YHWH yillachem lakhem ("the LORD will fight for you"), with the etnachta under yillachem creating a dramatic pause before the final clause ve'attem tacharishun ("and you shall be silent"). The cantillation on v. 21 gives special weight to beruach qadim azzah ("by a strong east wind"), highlighting the natural means through which the supernatural event is accomplished. The final verse (v. 31) is marked with distinctive cantillation that signals the climactic conclusion, with emphasis on vayya'aminu baYHWH uvMosheh avdo ("and they believed in the LORD and in Moses His servant").

The Codex Vaticanus pages 54–55 preserve the LXX text of Exodus 14. The text begins on page 54 (continuing from the end of chapter 13, which appeared on the same page) and extends through page 55. The Vaticanus text of chapter 14 follows the MT structure closely but with several characteristic LXX features. At v. 2, the LXX renders the place names as epenanti tēs epauleos ("opposite the encampment") for Pi-hahiroth, ana meson Magdōlou kai ana meson tēs thalassēs ("between Migdol and the sea"), and enantion Beelsepphōn ("before Beelsepphon") — note the variant spelling of Baal-zephon. At v. 3, the LXX renders nevukhim as planōntai houtoi en tē gē ("these are wandering in the land"), using planaō ("to wander, to be led astray"). At v. 4, the crucial hardening verb is rendered sklērunō tēn kardian Pharaō ("I will harden the heart of Pharaoh"), using the standard LXX hardening term. At v. 7, the LXX specifies tristatai epi pantōn ("captains over all"), using the term tristatēs (etymologically "third man," the chariot officer) to render shalishim. At v. 8, the LXX adds meta dunameōs pollēs ("with great power") for beyad ramah ("with a high hand"), interpreting the Hebrew idiom. At v. 13, the LXX renders yeshu'at YHWH as tēn sōtērian tēn para tou theou ("the salvation from God"), notably using tou theou ("of God") rather than kuriou ("of the Lord"). At v. 14, the LXX renders tacharishun as hēsuchasete ("you shall be quiet/still"), using hēsuchazō. At v. 20, the LXX appears to read the difficult Hebrew clause differently, rendering kai diēlthen hē nux ("and the night passed") where the MT has vayya'er et-hallaylah ("and it lit up the night"). At v. 21, the LXX renders beruach qadim azzah as en anemō notō biiaiō ("by a violent south wind"), reading qadim as "south" rather than "east" — a significant geographical reinterpretation. At v. 25, the LXX reads sunedēsen tous axonas tōn harmatōn autōn ("he bound the axles of their chariots") where the MT has vayyasar ("he turned aside/removed the wheels"), a notable variant that changes the mode of divine interference. At v. 27, the LXX renders le'etano as eis ton archaiontōpon ("to its former place"), interpreting the rare Hebrew term. At v. 31, the LXX renders vayya'aminu as episteusen ho laos tō theō ("the people believed God"), with some manuscript variants reading tō kuriō ("the Lord"). Throughout the Vaticanus text, the chapter division is marked by paragraph breaks in the continuous uncial script.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"They are wandering aimlessly in the land" (v. 3) — nevukhim hem ba'arets:** Hebrew ve'amar Par'oh livney Yisra'el nevukhim hem ba'arets sagar aleihem hammidbar. The participle nevukhim (from the root n-v-kh or b-v-kh, "to be confused, to wander, to be perplexed") describes a state of disoriented wandering — moving without direction or purpose. This is what Pharaoh is expected to conclude when he sees Israel's movements: rather than marching directly toward Canaan, they have turned back (v. 2, veyashuvu) and are camped between geographical obstacles (Migdol, the sea, Baal-zephon). Pharaoh will interpret the change of direction as confusion. The term occurs only here and in Esther 3:15 (where Susa is "confused/bewildered") and Joel 1:18 (where cattle are "perplexed"). The phrase sagar aleihem hammidbar ("the wilderness has closed in on them") uses sagar ("to shut, to close"), depicting the wilderness as a trap that has snapped shut around the Israelites. Pharaoh sees them as cornered prey. The irony is that this is precisely what God intended — the apparent vulnerability is a divine trap, not for Israel but for Pharaoh. LOGOS renders "wandering aimlessly" for nevukhim and "closed in on them" for sagar aleihem. The LXX renders planōntai houtoi en tē gē ("these are wandering in the land"), using planaō ("to wander, to go astray"), and sunekleisen gar autous hē erēmos ("for the wilderness has shut them in"), using sunkleiō ("to shut in together, to enclose").

2. **"And I will strengthen Pharaoh's heart" (v. 4) — vechizzaqti et-lev Par'oh:** Hebrew vachizzaqti et-lev Par'oh veradaf acharehem ve'ikkavdah bePar'oh uvkhol-cheilo veyad'u Mitsrayim ki-ani YHWH. The verb chizzaqti (Piel perfect of chazaq with consecutive vav, functioning as imperfect) means "I will strengthen, I will make firm, I will make resolute." This is the Piel form with God as the explicit agent — the divine hardening that has been announced since 4:21 and 7:3. The purpose clause is explicit: God strengthens Pharaoh's heart so that Pharaoh will pursue (veradaf), and through this pursuit God will be glorified (ve'ikkavdah, Niphal of kaved, "to be heavy, to be glorious"). The root k-v-d ("heavy, glorious") connects to the kaved ("heavy") heart of earlier hardening passages — the very heaviness that defined Pharaoh's stubborn heart now becomes the glory (kavod) of God. God's glory is demonstrated precisely through the defeat of the one whose heart He made heavy. The knowledge formula veyad'u Mitsrayim ki-ani YHWH ("and the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD") states the theological purpose of the entire Sea event: the Egyptians themselves will recognize YHWH's identity. LOGOS renders "strengthen" for chizzaqti and "be glorified" for ve'ikkavdah, preserving the k-v-d root connection. The LXX renders sklērunō tēn kardian Pharaō ("I will harden the heart of Pharaoh") and endoxasthēsomai en Pharaō ("I will be glorified in Pharaoh"), using endoxazō for the Niphal of kaved.

3. **"With a high hand" (v. 8) — beyad ramah:** Hebrew uveney Yisra'el yots'im beyad ramah. The phrase beyad ramah (literally "with a raised/lifted hand") is an idiom for boldness, defiance, and open confidence. Israel departed Egypt not as fugitives sneaking away but with visible boldness — their hand (metaphorically, their posture and bearing) was "high" or "raised." The same phrase appears in Numbers 15:30 for sinning "with a high hand" (defiantly, presumptuously) and in Numbers 33:3 for the Exodus departure. The contrast with the Egyptians' perception in v. 3 (nevukhim, "wandering aimlessly") is deliberate: Israel goes out boldly, but Pharaoh will misread their encampment as confused wandering. LOGOS renders "with a high hand" to preserve the Hebrew idiom. The LXX renders en cheiri hupsēlē ("with a high hand"), translating literally, though at v. 8 the Vaticanus appears to read meta dunameōs pollēs ("with great power"), interpreting the idiom.

4. **"Stand firm and see the salvation of the LORD" (v. 13) — hityatsvu ure'u et-yeshu'at YHWH:** Hebrew al-tira'u hityatsvu ure'u et-yeshu'at YHWH asher-ya'aseh lakhem hayyom ki asher re'item et-Mitsrayim hayyom lo tosifu lir'otam od ad-olam. Moses' response to Israel's terror contains three imperatives: al-tira'u ("do not fear"), hityatsvu ("stand firm," Hithpael of yatsav, "to station oneself, to take one's stand"), and ure'u ("and see"). The command is to cease panicking, plant one's feet, and observe what God is about to do. The noun yeshu'ah ("salvation, deliverance") from the root y-sh-', the same root that gives the name Yehoshua/Joshua ("the LORD saves") and ultimately Yeshua/Jesus. This is the first occurrence of yeshu'ah in the Torah — the first time the concept of divine "salvation" is named. The promise lo tosifu lir'otam od ad-olam ("you will not again see them ever, unto eternity") is absolute and permanent: the Egypt that marches toward them today will never threaten them again. The verb tosifu (Hiphil of yasaf, "to add, to do again") with a following infinitive means "you will not do again to see them." LOGOS renders "stand firm" for hityatsvu, "salvation" for yeshu'ah, and "unto eternity" for ad-olam. The LXX renders tēn sōtērian tēn para tou theou ("the salvation from God"), substituting tou theou for YHWH.

5. **"And you shall be silent" (v. 14) — ve'attem tacharishun:** Hebrew YHWH yillachem lakhem ve'attem tacharishun. The verb tacharishun (Hiphil of charash, "to be silent, to be still, to hold one's peace") commands Israel to cease all speech — their complaints (vv. 11–12) and their cries (v. 10). The imperative is stark: the LORD will fight (yillachem, Niphal of lacham, "to fight"), and your role is silence. The juxtaposition is theologically loaded: divine warfare requires human passivity. Israel's contribution to the battle is not action but trust expressed as silence. This is the quintessential statement of holy war theology: God is the warrior; the people are spectators. The Niphal yillachem indicates that the LORD Himself enters combat — the battle is His, not Israel's. LOGOS renders "be silent" for tacharishun and "will fight" for yillachem. The LXX renders hēsuchasete ("you shall be quiet/still"), using a term that emphasizes stillness and rest.

6. **"Lift up your staff and stretch out your hand over the sea, and divide it" (v. 16) — harem et-mattekha uneteh et-yadekha al-hayyam uveqa'ehu:** Hebrew ve'attah harem et-mattekha uneteh et-yadekha al-hayyam uveqa'ehu veyavo'u veney-Yisra'el betokh hayyam bayyabbashah. The sequence of imperatives moves from passivity to action: after telling Israel to stand still (v. 13), God now tells Moses to act. Three verbs: harem ("lift up," Hiphil imperative of rum), neteh ("stretch out," Qal imperative of natah), and veqa'ehu ("and divide/split it," Qal imperative of baqa' with 3ms suffix). The verb baqa' ("to split, to cleave, to divide") is a powerful physical term — it describes splitting wood (Gen. 22:3), cleaving a rock to bring water (Ps. 78:15; Isa. 48:21), and rending the earth (Num. 16:31). When applied to water, it evokes the primordial division of the waters in creation (though Genesis 1 uses badal rather than baqa'). The staff (matteh) is the same rod that turned into a serpent (4:2–4), struck the Nile (7:20), and extended over Egypt during the plagues. It is the instrument of divine power wielded through human agency. LOGOS renders "divide" for baqa'. The LXX renders rēxon autēn ("tear it apart"), using rhēgnumi ("to break, to rend, to tear"), a more violent term.

7. **"The angel of God... journeyed and went behind them" (v. 19) — vayyissa mal'akh ha'Elohim haholekh lifney machaneh Yisra'el vayyeylekh me'acharehem:** The angel of God (mal'akh ha'Elohim) is distinguished from but closely identified with the pillar of cloud — both move simultaneously from front to rear. The participle haholekh ("the one going") links this angel to the LORD who was holekh ("going") before them in 13:21. Whether the angel and the pillar are the same entity described from different perspectives, or two distinct manifestations, is debated. The text presents them in parallel: the angel journeyed (vayyissa, from nasa', "to pull up [stakes], to journey") from before the camp and went behind; the pillar of cloud journeyed (vayyissa) from before them and stood (vayyaammod) behind them. The effect is a divine rearguard: what led Israel now shields Israel. LOGOS renders "the angel of God" and preserves the parallel movement. The LXX renders ho angelos tou theou ho proporeuomenos tēs parembolēs tōn huiōn Israēl ("the angel of God who was going before the camp of the sons of Israel").

8. **"And there was the cloud and the darkness, and it lit up the night" (v. 20) — vayehi he'anan vehachoshekh vayya'er et-hallaylah:** This is one of the most difficult clauses in the chapter. The Hebrew reads literally: "and-it-was the-cloud and-the-darkness and-it-lit-up [direct object marker] the-night." The subject of vayya'er ("and it lit up") is ambiguous — does the cloud illuminate the night (for Israel), or does the pillar of fire illuminate the night? The context suggests that the cloud-and-darkness side faced Egypt (keeping them in darkness), while the fire side faced Israel (giving them light). The clause velo-qarav zeh el-zeh kol-hallaylah ("and the one did not come near the other all the night") describes the two camps kept apart by the divine barrier. The LXX apparently read the verse differently, rendering kai diēlthen hē nux ("and the night passed"), which may reflect a different Vorlage or an interpretive simplification of the difficult Hebrew. LOGOS preserves the MT reading. The interplay of cloud, darkness, and light in this single verse recapitulates the primordial separation of light from darkness in Genesis 1:4 — God once again divides light from darkness, this time not cosmologically but salvifically: light for His people, darkness for their enemies.

9. **"By a strong east wind all the night" (v. 21) — beruach qadim azzah kol-hallaylah:** Hebrew vayyeyt Mosheh et-yado al-hayyam vayyolekh YHWH et-hayyam beruach qadim azzah kol-hallaylah vayyasem et-hayyam lecharavah vayyibbaq'u hammayim. The divine agency operates through natural means: the LORD caused the sea to go (vayyolekh, Hiphil of halakh, "to cause to walk/go") by means of a ruach qadim azzah ("a strong east wind"). The noun ruach means both "wind" and "spirit" — the same word that hovered over the waters in Genesis 1:2 (ruach Elohim merachefet al-peney hammayim). The adjective qadim ("eastern") indicates a wind from the east; in the geography of the exodus route, an east wind blowing across one of the shallow lakes in the isthmus could indeed drive the water aside. The adjective azzah ("strong, fierce") is the feminine form of az, agreeing with the feminine noun ruach. The effect is described in three clauses: the LORD caused the sea to go (vayyolekh), He made the sea into dry land (vayyasem et-hayyam lecharavah), and the waters were divided (vayyibbaq'u, Niphal of baqa'). The term charavah ("dry land, dry ground") differs from yabbashah ("dry ground") used in v. 22 — charavah emphasizes the dryness as a result of water withdrawal, while yabbashah denotes solid dry earth. Together, the two terms emphasize the completeness of the transformation: the sea bed becomes as firm and dry as land. LOGOS renders "east wind" for ruach qadim and "dry land" for charavah. The LXX renders en anemō notō biiaiō ("by a violent south wind"), reading notō ("south") for qadim ("east") — a striking geographical reinterpretation, possibly reflecting the LXX translator's understanding of the local geography from an Egyptian perspective, or a different textual tradition.

10. **"The waters were a wall to them on their right and on their left" (v. 22) — vehamayim lahem chomah miminam umissemolam:** The noun chomah ("wall") describes the waters standing on either side of the path as solid, vertical barriers — not merely heaped water but walls. This image transcends any naturalistic explanation: walls imply solidity, structure, and architectural permanence. The water has been transformed from its natural fluid state into a construction that serves the passage of God's people. The same phrase repeats verbatim in v. 29, forming an inclusio around the entire crossing narrative: the walls of water are the first and last thing described about Israel's path through the sea. Some ancient interpreters (and one Masoretic tradition preserved in a few manuscripts) have read chamah ("wrath, heat") instead of chomah ("wall"), yielding "the waters were wrath/heat to them" — but the standard Masoretic reading chomah ("wall") is strongly supported and is followed by LOGOS. The LXX renders to de hudōr autois teichos ek dexiōn kai teichos ex euōnumōn ("the water was to them a wall on the right and a wall on the left"), using teichos ("wall"), translating literally and even doubling the noun for emphasis.

11. **"In the morning watch the LORD looked down" (v. 24) — vayehi be'ashmoret habboqer vayyashqef YHWH el-machaneh Mitsrayim:** The ashmoret habboqer ("morning watch") is the last of the three night watches in the ancient Israelite system — roughly from 2:00 AM to 6:00 AM (cf. Judg. 7:19, which mentions the "middle watch"; Lam. 2:19, the "beginning of the watches"). The verb vayyashqef (Hiphil of shaqaf, "to look down from above, to gaze down, to overlook") implies a downward gaze from an elevated position — God looks down from the pillar of fire and cloud upon the Egyptian camp below. This verb is used of God looking down from heaven (Ps. 14:2; 102:20) and of looking down from a window (Gen. 26:8; 2 Sam. 6:16). The combination of looking and confusing (vayyahom, from hamam, "to throw into confusion, to discomfit, to panic") follows the pattern of holy war: God observes the enemy, then strikes with supernatural confusion. The verb hamam is the characteristic holy war verb (cf. Josh. 10:10; Judg. 4:15; 1 Sam. 7:10), describing the divine panic that renders armies helpless. LOGOS renders "morning watch" for ashmoret habboqer, "looked down" for vayyashqef, and "threw into confusion" for vayyahom. The LXX renders en tē phulakē tē eōthinē ("in the early morning watch") and kateblepsen kurios epi tēn parembolēn tōn Aiguptiōn ("the Lord looked down upon the camp of the Egyptians").

12. **"He turned aside the wheels of his chariots" (v. 25) — vayyasar et-ofan markevotav:** Hebrew vayyasar et-ofan markevotav vaynahagehu bikhvedut. The verb vayyasar (from sur, "to turn aside, to remove, to take away") indicates that God caused the chariot wheels to come off or to swerve aside — a mechanical failure that disabled the Egyptian pursuit. The noun ofan ("wheel," singular used collectively) is the chariot wheel. The verb vaynahagehu (Piel of nahag, "to drive, to lead") with bikhvedut ("with heaviness, with difficulty") describes the Egyptians struggling to drive their chariots after the wheels are disrupted. The noun khvedut ("heaviness") is from the same root k-v-d that has been the governing metaphor of the entire plague narrative — Pharaoh's "heavy" heart now manifests as the "heaviness" of his disabled chariots. The wordplay is devastatingly ironic: the kaved (heaviness/glory) that defined Pharaoh's resistance now physically impedes his army, while the same root provides God's kavod (glory). The Kethiv reading is vayyasar ("he removed"), while some manuscripts and the Samaritan Pentateuch read vayyasset ("he bound/locked"), and the LXX renders sunedēsen ("he bound together"), suggesting a variant tradition in which God locked the wheels rather than removed them. LOGOS follows the Kethiv/MT reading "turned aside." The LXX rendering sunedēsen tous axonas tōn harmatōn autōn ("he bound the axles of their chariots") is noted as a significant variant.

13. **"The sea returned at the turning of the morning to its normal flow" (v. 27) — vayyashov hayyam lifnot boqer le'etano:** Hebrew vayyeyt Mosheh et-yado al-hayyam vayyashov hayyam lifnot boqer le'etano uMitsrayim nassim liqra'to vayena'er YHWH et-Mitsrayim betokh hayyam. The verb vayyashov ("and it returned") from shuv describes the sea's restoration to its natural state. The phrase lifnot boqer ("at the turning of the morning") is a temporal marker meaning "at the approach of dawn" — the same dawn that began with the morning watch (v. 24). The crucial term le'etano (from eitan, "perennial, enduring, constant") means "to its perpetual state, to its normal flow, to its enduring condition." The sea returns to what it always was — the miraculous interruption was temporary; the natural order reasserts itself. The verb vayena'er (Piel of na'ar, "to shake off, to shake out") is strikingly physical: the LORD "shook off" or "shook out" the Egyptians into the sea, as one shakes insects from a garment. This verb occurs in Nehemiah 5:13, where Nehemiah shakes out his garment as a symbolic curse. The image reduces the mighty Egyptian army to something casually discarded. LOGOS renders "to its normal flow" for le'etano and "shook off" for vayena'er. The LXX renders eis ton archaion topon ("to its former place") for le'etano and exetinaxen kurios tous Aiguptious meson tēs thalassēs ("the Lord shook out the Egyptians into the midst of the sea") for vayena'er, using ektinassō ("to shake out/off").

14. **"The LORD saved Israel on that day" (v. 30) — vayyosha YHWH bayyom hahu et-Yisra'el miyyad Mitsrayim:** The verb vayyosha (Hiphil of yasha', "to save, to deliver") is the verbal form of the noun yeshu'ah ("salvation") from v. 13. Moses promised Israel would see "the salvation of the LORD" (yeshu'at YHWH); now the narrator reports that "the LORD saved" (vayyosha YHWH). The promise and its fulfillment use the same root y-sh-'. The phrase miyyad Mitsrayim ("from the hand of Egypt") is ironic: Israel went out beyad ramah ("with a high hand," v. 8), and now they are saved from the yad ("hand") of Egypt. The hand of Israel was raised in confidence; the hand of Egypt is broken. LOGOS renders "saved" for vayyosha. The LXX renders errusato kurios ton Israēl ("the Lord rescued Israel"), using rhuomai ("to rescue, to deliver").

15. **"The people feared the LORD, and they believed in the LORD and in Moses His servant" (v. 31) — vayyir'u ha'am et-YHWH vayya'aminu baYHWH uvMosheh avdo:** Hebrew vayyar Yisra'el et-hayyad haggedolah asher asah YHWH beMitsrayim vayyir'u ha'am et-YHWH vayya'aminu baYHWH uvMosheh avdo. The chapter concludes with a three-part sequence: seeing (vayyar, "and he/they saw"), fearing (vayyir'u, "and they feared"), and believing (vayya'aminu, Hiphil of aman, "and they believed/trusted"). Seeing the great hand leads to fearing, and fearing leads to believing. The verb he'emin (Hiphil of aman, "to trust, to believe, to consider reliable") is a covenantal term — it denotes not mere intellectual assent but relational trust, the placing of one's confidence in another's reliability. The root aman gives us "amen" ("so be it, truly") and emunah ("faithfulness, steadfastness"). When Israel "believed in the LORD" (vayya'aminu baYHWH), they acknowledged His reliability as demonstrated by the Sea event. The addition of uvMosheh avdo ("and in Moses His servant") establishes Moses' authority on the basis of the sea crossing — his legitimacy as mediator is confirmed by the event he announced (v. 13) and God performed (v. 21). The term eved ("servant") is a title of honor in the Hebrew Bible: Abraham (Gen. 26:24), Moses (Num. 12:7; Josh. 1:2), David (2 Sam. 7:5), and the Suffering Servant (Isa. 52:13) all bear it. LOGOS renders "feared" for vayyir'u and "believed" for vayya'aminu. The LXX renders ephobēthē ho laos ton kurion kai episteusan tō theō kai Mōusē tō theraponti autou ("the people feared the Lord and believed God and Moses His attendant"), using therapōn ("attendant, minister") rather than doulos ("slave") for eved — a term Homer uses for trusted companions, and which the LXX reserves almost exclusively for Moses (cf. Heb. 3:5).

### Hardening Verb Tracking — Exodus 14

| Verse | Hebrew | Verb/Root | Form | Agent | LOGOS Rendering |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 14:4 | vachizzaqti et-lev Par'oh | chazaq | Piel (1cs perfect + vav consec.) | God ("I will strengthen") | "I will strengthen Pharaoh's heart" |
| 14:8 | vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh melekh Mitsrayim | chazaq | Piel (3ms imperfect + vav consec.) | God (the LORD strengthened) | "the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh" |
| 14:17 | va'ani hinni mechazzeq et-lev Mitsrayim | chazaq | Piel (ms participle) | God ("I am strengthening") | "I am strengthening the heart of the Egyptians" |

*Note: All three hardening verbs in Exodus 14 use the same root chazaq in the Piel stem, and all three have God as the explicit agent. This is the final cluster of hardening verbs in the Exodus narrative, and it is theologically decisive: at the Sea of Reeds, there is no ambiguity about agency. God Himself strengthens Pharaoh's heart (v. 4, announced; v. 8, narrated) and the hearts of all the Egyptians (v. 17, announced). The Piel form is causative — God actively causes the hardening. The purpose is stated twice: ve'ikkavdah ("and I will be glorified," vv. 4, 17). The root k-v-d ("heavy/glorious") that described Pharaoh's stubborn heart (kaved, 7:14; 8:15, 28; 9:7, 34) now describes God's glory manifest through Pharaoh's destruction. The heaviness that was Pharaoh's sin becomes the glory that is God's vindication. After 14:17, the hardening verb sequence that began at 4:21 is complete: God's announced purpose — "I will harden Pharaoh's heart" — has been fulfilled, and the knowledge formula — "the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD" (14:4, 18) — provides the theological conclusion. The progression across Exodus 4–14 shows 20 instances of hardening verbs: roughly equal distribution between divine agency and Pharaoh's self-hardening in chapters 4–11, resolving into exclusively divine agency in chapter 14. The theological implication is that Pharaoh's initial self-hardening became the occasion for God's judicial hardening, which in turn served the larger purpose of divine self-revelation.*

---

### Theological Note: The Sea as Judgment and Salvation

Exodus 14 is the climax of the Exodus narrative — the event toward which the plagues, the Passover, and the departure have been building. The chapter divides into five movements: (1) the divine trap (vv. 1–4), (2) the Egyptian pursuit (vv. 5–9), (3) the crisis and the promise (vv. 10–14), (4) the crossing (vv. 15–22), and (5) the destruction and the faith (vv. 23–31). Each movement develops the central theological claim: the same event that saves Israel destroys Egypt.

The architecture of the chapter is built on a series of reversals. Pharaoh perceives Israel as confused (nevukhim, v. 3), but the confusion is God's design. Pharaoh pursues with military confidence (v. 6–9), but the pursuer becomes the prey. Israel cries out in terror (v. 10), but their terror gives way to the silence of trust (v. 14). The sea that appears to be Israel's final obstacle becomes their path of salvation and Egypt's grave. The east wind that divides the waters (v. 21) and the returning waters that cover the army (vv. 27–28) are the same body of water acting in two opposite modes — as deliverance and as judgment — simultaneously.

The hardening theology reaches its culmination in this chapter. Three times the Piel of chazaq appears (vv. 4, 8, 17), each time with God as the explicit agent and the purpose of divine glorification stated. The question that has haunted the plague narrative — is God unjust in hardening Pharaoh's heart? — is answered not by philosophical argument but by the event itself. The hardening serves the kavod ("glory") of God, and the kavod of God is demonstrated in the deliverance of the oppressed and the defeat of the oppressor. The root k-v-d performs double duty throughout: Pharaoh's heart was kaved ("heavy") in resistance, and God is nikbad ("glorified") in judgment. The very stubbornness that Pharaoh displayed becomes the instrument through which God's character is revealed to the nations: veyad'u Mitsrayim ki-ani YHWH ("and the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD," vv. 4, 18).

The chapter's central command — hityatsvu ure'u et-yeshu'at YHWH ("stand firm and see the salvation of the LORD," v. 13) — introduces a concept that will reverberate through the entire biblical tradition. This is the first use of yeshu'ah ("salvation") in the Torah, and it defines salvation not as human achievement but as divine action witnessed by humans. Israel's role is not to fight but to stand, see, and be silent (tacharishun, v. 14). The LORD Himself is the warrior (yillachem, v. 14), and human agency is limited to Moses' outstretched hand — itself only the instrument of divine command (v. 16). This pattern of divine initiative and human witness becomes paradigmatic for Israel's understanding of salvation throughout the Hebrew Bible (cf. 2 Chr. 20:17, where Jehoshaphat echoes Moses' words almost verbatim).

The physical details of the crossing resist both pure naturalism and pure supernaturalism. The "strong east wind all the night" (v. 21) describes a natural phenomenon — wind-driven water displacement is well attested in shallow bodies of water in the Suez isthmus region. Yet the "walls" of water (chomah, vv. 22, 29), the precise timing, the selective effect (Israel passes safely; Egypt is destroyed), and the divine command-and-response structure all transcend natural explanation. The text holds together the natural and the supernatural: God uses wind, but the wind accomplishes what no wind could accomplish on its own. The charavah ("dry land," v. 21) and yabbashah ("dry ground," vv. 22, 29) employ two distinct terms to emphasize the completeness of the transformation — the sea bed is not merely exposed but solidified into walkable terrain.

The chapter's conclusion (vv. 30–31) establishes a three-step response to divine salvation: seeing (vayyar, the physical evidence of Egyptian corpses on the shore), fearing (vayyir'u, the awe-filled recognition of divine power), and believing (vayya'aminu, the covenantal trust that issues from witnessing God's faithfulness). This sequence — sight to fear to faith — becomes a paradigm for Israel's relationship with God. Faith is not blind; it arises from witnessed events. But the events alone are insufficient without the interpretive framework: Israel must see not merely dead Egyptians but "the great hand which the LORD had done" (hayyad haggedolah asher asah YHWH, v. 31). The hand of God — the same yad that was "strong" (chazaqah) in the plagues (3:19; 6:1; 13:3, 9, 14, 16) and "high" (ramah) in Israel's departure (v. 8) — is now "great" (gedolah) in its climactic manifestation. The progression from strong to high to great traces the trajectory of divine power from oppression through liberation to consummation.

The faith of v. 31 is simultaneously the theological climax and the narrative setup for the Song of the Sea (chapter 15). Israel believed — but this faith is fragile, as the murmuring narratives of chapters 15–17 will immediately demonstrate. The Sea event is both the greatest demonstration of divine power in the Torah and the most quickly forgotten. The tension between v. 31's faith and the complaints that follow defines the wilderness narrative: Israel has seen the salvation of the LORD, yet cannot sustain the trust that seeing produced.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 14 (pages 54–55) preserves the LXX version with several notable features. At v. 2, the LXX renders the place names with characteristic Greek adaptations: Pi-hahiroth becomes epenanti tēs epauleos ("opposite the encampment"), suggesting the translator understood the Hebrew as a descriptive phrase rather than a proper name. At v. 7, the LXX renders shalishim as tristatai ("captains, third-men"), preserving the etymological connection to "three" (the officer who stood as the third man in the chariot alongside the driver and the warrior). At v. 9, the LXX adds kai hē hippoi ("and the cavalry"), expanding the military catalog. At v. 13, the LXX renders yeshu'at YHWH as tēn sōtērian tēn para tou theou ("the salvation from God"), using theou rather than kuriou — a substitution that may reflect the Vaticanus scribe's tendencies at this point. At v. 20, the LXX reading kai diēlthen hē nux ("and the night passed") for the MT's vayya'er et-hallaylah ("and it lit up the night") represents one of the most significant divergences in the chapter — the LXX appears to have read or interpreted the Hebrew differently, producing a less theologically loaded clause. At v. 21, the LXX's rendering of ruach qadim as anemos notos ("south wind") is geographically notable: from the perspective of someone standing at the northern end of the Gulf of Suez, a south wind would blow the water northward, while an east wind would blow it westward. The LXX translator may have been working with a different geographical understanding of the crossing site, or the term qadim may have had a broader directional meaning in the Vorlage. At v. 25, the LXX's sunedēsen ("he bound together") for the MT's vayyasar ("he turned aside/removed") represents a different understanding of how God disabled the chariots: binding/locking the axles versus removing the wheels. Both traditions describe divine sabotage, but through different mechanisms. At v. 27, the LXX renders le'etano as eis ton archaion topon ("to its former/ancient place"), interpreting the rare word eitan ("perennial, enduring") as "ancient/original." At v. 31, the LXX uses therapōn ("attendant") for eved ("servant") — a term the LXX reserves almost exclusively for Moses (cf. Num. 12:7 LXX; Heb. 3:5), distinguishing Moses from ordinary "servants" (douloi) and marking him as a trusted companion of the divine household. Throughout pages 54–55, the Vaticanus text maintains close structural fidelity to the MT while exhibiting the characteristic LXX features of lexical adaptation, occasional geographical reinterpretation, and theological clarification.

--- LOGOS
